If you feel adventurous check my Google reviews for those 2 places on how to get there.

text_clean: En el Museo de Sitio Chan Chan, que está muy cerca del complejo arqueológico “Chan Chan”, se encuentran resguardadas y exhibidas ​muchas piezas arqueológicas de cerámica, piezas líticas y de metales, textiles y restos óseos provenientes del complejo arqueológico y sus alrededores. Una de las cosas más interesantes para apreciar en el museo es una enorme maqueta que recrea las posibles dimensiones de la ciudad de “Chan Chan”, fue una ciudad precolombina de adobe, construida cerca a la costa por los chimúes. También es la ciudad construida en adobe más grande de América​ y la segunda en el mundo.

I personally would recommend getting a tour guide as there is alot to learn from these 2000 year old ruins. However, it's also nice having a wander round as these ruins are so spectacular.

text_clean: Watching the Nazca Lines from the skies is a one in a lifetime opportunity. There are so many lines that the tours have chosen to show only the most impressive ones. Despite numerous theories, so far no one has been able to understand why these lines were marked on the soil as they are only perceptible from the sky. The lines were amazing to see, the flight less enjoyable as it was tourning a lot. Three out of five passagers got sick... I also wished that the flight was conducted a bit closer to the ground to improve visibility of the lines. It's probably a safety measure though.

text_clean: Sacsayhuamán is genuinely impressive. The sheer size of the stones and the fact that these structures have lasted this long make you stop and think about the level of planning, labour, and motivation it must have taken to build them. Part of the experience included walking through a pitch-black corridor carved into the rock, which was both cool and slightly terrifying. One of the more impactful sites in the area, especially if you’re choosing carefully which ruins to visit. Martin Boyce Barbados

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: positivo

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: positivo

El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

text_clean: As of a few weeks ago because they’re busy making repairs after a colapse, it opened back up and we were allowed to view the outside of one of the entrances, but not go inside…. yet I found it worth it for the journey up that demonstrated how steep and inaccessible the place is to get to being 10,000 feet up. After seeing the ruins and taking that 1 kilometer hike up to narrow entrance (one of three), I enjoyed how I could now notice bits of ruins and the edge of the fortress peeking out from above on my way down while in the cable car to try to imagine what the Inca saw when going up and taking over the Cloud people… it surely deserves five stars when fully open. … but still worth the money and worth exploring north Peru

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.
label: positivo

text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. input_modelo: aspecto: atractivos resena: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

text_clean: Incredible experience! Our guide, Jordan, was knowledgeable, and really tailored his tour to our interests. We took the Inca Bridge option, and was so pleased with how little people were on the trail. We got to see more of the orchids, insects ( especially the butterflies) and hummingbirds up close. The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"
label: positivo

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: negativo

text_clean: As of a few weeks ago because they’re busy making repairs after a colapse, it opened back up and we were allowed to view the outside of one of the entrances, but not go inside…. yet I found it worth it for the journey up that demonstrated how steep and inaccessible the place is to get to being 10,000 feet up. After seeing the ruins and taking that 1 kilometer hike up to narrow entrance (one of three), I enjoyed how I could now notice bits of ruins and the edge of the fortress peeking out from above on my way down while in the cable car to try to imagine what the Inca saw when going up and taking over the Cloud people… it surely deserves five stars when fully open. … but still worth the money and worth exploring north Peru

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.
label: negativo

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.
label: neutro

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: positivo

One of the more impactful sites in the area, especially if you’re choosing carefully which ruins to visit. Martin Boyce Barbados

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: positivo

text_clean: La concepción del museo no corresponde al sitio arqueológico, no solo porque es minúsculo en comparación a toda el área en la que están los templos, sino porque mantiene en almacén gran parte de los objetos y cuerpos hallados, no se exhiben. Su infraestructura es inadecuada por insuficiente y al 27/07/2024, luce muy descuidada por falta de mantenimiento, señalización y limpieza. Altamente recomendable contratar guía para el museo y el sitio arqueológico (especialmente), para comprender que incluso lo que se expone y preserva en el sitio, es una pequeña fracción de Chan Chan. destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.

text_clean: Saqsaywaman absolutely blew me away — the scale of this place is hard to comprehend until you’re standing right next to those massive stones. 🏔️ The precision of the Inca construction is incredible, especially knowing it was built without modern tools. The views over Cusco are stunning, and it’s a great spot to take a walk, explore, and really feel the history around you. It’s less crowded than Machu Picchu, which makes the experience more relaxed and enjoyable. I’m giving it 4 stars because there’s not a lot of information on-site unless you have a guide, and it can feel a bit underwhelming if you don’t know the history beforehand. But overall, it’s definitely worth visiting — especially if you’re already in Cusco and want something impressive without a full-day trip

El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: We had a terrible experience here. It is very hard to find any valuable descriptions online so we wanted to hire a guide. The place in the afternoon is a no-go. It is overcrowded with tourists, you cannot enjoy anything. The sun shines from behind the hill, so for one, the site is already in shade, which is not pretty, and it shines in your face so you cannot see anything. Don’t be like us, go in the morning. Worst historical site visit I ever had.

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: negativo

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: neutro

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...

The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: neutro

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Probably a nice museum - if you understand Spanish perfectly. Only in the beginning there were some English translations but then everything is only in Spanish. So without understanding anything there were some nice jewelry and pottery but also these were almost always the same. According to the guest book at the end there are almost only Perú citizens visiting this site. I guess more foreigners would come if they’d understand anything. Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

text_clean: All in all, what you see from the outside is what you get. As Canadians visiting in February, we made the grave error of visiting during the heat of the day. This place is a hot hellscape on a Peruvian summer day. Only accessible during a tour, the location offers no respite from the sun. The tour takes a full hour, which, while very interesting and educational, is a bit of a long time to be looking at bricks. Our tour guide was very good, and we enjoyed learning about Lima culture, but there isn't much to see other than the clay mud bricks and the views from the top, which are definitely quite good. If you plan to visit, go during the evening.

text_clean: You can’t beat the price for this attraction. What could use vast improvement is the mandatory guided tour, at least the guide we were paired with. The amplification system used was not adequate for the number of people in our group. But the worst was the scripted, lacklustre delivery of the tour. Our guide spoke in a monotone, never deviated from the script and hardly ever engaged with the crowd. Her comment at the end about tips being appreciated was almost laughable. I didn’t see any money change hands. The museum should increase the entrance fee and invest in better guides who add value to this incredible site.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. Sí antes de venderme la entrada me hubieran dicho "señor estamos próximos a cerrar, tome en cuenta q es probable que el tiempo no le sea suficiente" ya sabría a lo q me estaba lanzando. Sí van a ir, calculen 2 hrs para admirar todo, para procesar el arte de cada obra y no ir al museo solo por entrar y ya.
label: neutro
